coredata.dc:description: High intensity ultrasound (HIU) offers an alternative to traditional methods of food preservation, and is regarded as a green and promising emerging technology. Ultrasound generates acoustic cavitation in a liquid medium, developing physical forces that are considered the main mechanism responsible for changes in exposed materials. In meat, ultrasound has been successfully used to improve processes such as mass transfer and marination, tenderization of meat and inactivation of microorganisms. It is also an alternative to traditional meat ageing methods for improving the quality properties of meat. Moreover, the combination of ultrasonic energy with a sanitizing agent can improve the effect of microbial reduction in foods. This review describes recent potential applications of ultrasound in meat systems, as well as physical and chemical effects of ultrasound treatment on the conservation and modification of processed meat foods. Finally, the ultrasound application parameters must be deep explored and established before the method can be scaled to industrial levels.

head.ce:title: Ultrasound and meat quality: A review

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High intensity ultrasound (HIU) offers an alternative to traditional methods of food preservation, and is regarded as a green and promising emerging technology. Ultrasound generates acoustic cavitation in a liquid medium, developing physical forces that are considered the main mechanism responsible for changes in exposed materials. In meat, ultrasound has been successfully used to improve processes such as mass transfer and marination, tenderization of meat and inactivation of microorganisms. It is also an alternative to traditional meat ageing methods for improving the quality properties of meat. Moreover, the combination of ultrasonic energy with a sanitizing agent can improve the effect of microbial reduction in foods. This review describes recent potential applications of ultrasound in meat systems, as well as physical and chemical effects of ultrasound treatment on the conservation and modification of processed meat foods. Finally, the ultrasound application parameters must be deep explored and established before the method can be scaled to industrial levels.

ce:section.ce:para: Evolution of food processes is driven by changes in consumer preferences and the need to produce safe and high-quality foods. Emerging technologies appeared to be the best alternative to achieve the mentioned characteristics. These technologies, which include the use of high pressure, electrical pulses, micro-filtration, and ultrasonication, are especially designed for economy, simplicity, and energy-efficiency. Besides that, ultrasound used in the “Green Food Processing” is used to ensure high quality and safe food [1]. Ultrasound is acoustic energy [2], therefore, it is a non-ionizing, non-invasive, and non-polluting form of mechanical energy [3]. It is considered an emerging method with a great potential to control, improve, and accelerate processes without damaging the quality of food [4,5]. A low-power and high-frequency method is used to monitor the composition and physicochemical properties of food components and products during processing. However, in recent years, research studies have been focused on assessing the effects of ultrasound on processes including mass transfer or marinating, meat tenderizing, crystallization, freezing, drying, emulsification, homogenization, and inactivation of microorganisms [3]. Although ultrasound waves have been used to improve a wide variety of characteristics for a variety of matrices and processes [5,6], the scaling of ultrasonic application up to industrial levels have been established for a relatively small number of processes [4]. As emphasized by Chemat and Ashokkumar [1], a key goal of ultrasound research is to study and analyse both desirable and undesirable degradation phenomena in foods resulting from ultrasonic treatment. This need has particular importance in the meat sector. The objective of this review is to associate the effect of ultrasound with the major quality characteristics of fresh meat. We believe the results will contribute to establish the appropriate conditions for the scaling-up of ultrasonic technology to the industrial level.

Ultrasound is a form of energy generated by a longitudinal mechanical wave whose vibration frequency is greater than 20,000 cycles per second (20 kHz), which is above the audible limit for humans. Sound is considered a pressure wave with one-dimensional propagation. The speed of an ultrasonic pulse depends on the acoustic properties of the medium, and the speed of sound propagation is greater in solids than in liquids and higher in liquids than in gases [7]. In an ultrasound system, the electrical energy is transformed into vibrational energy, which is mechanical energy [8] that has been transmitted through a sonicated medium. Part of the input energy is lost through conversion to heat, and the rest can produce cavitation. A fraction of the cavitation energy produces chemical, physical, or biological effects while other fractions are reflected and consumed in the re-emission of sound. Ultrasound ranges from 20 kHz to 10 MHz and is divided into three categories: 1) high-intensity (>5 W/cm2 or 10–1000 W/cm2) and low-frequency (20–100 kHz); 2) medium-intensity and intermediate-frequency (100 kHz–1 MHz); and 3) low-intensity (<1 W/cm2) and high-frequency (1–10 MHz) [9]. Three different methods are used to apply ultrasound to products: a) direct application; b) coupling to the device, and c) immersion in an ultrasound bath [3].

Low-intensity, high-frequency ultrasound has analytical applications that provide information about the physicochemical properties of foods such as composition, structure, and condition [10]. Furthermore, unlike conventional analytical techniques, it is non-invasive and non-destructive [11] and the measurements are fast, automated, and easy to use in both laboratories and production lines. High-power ultrasound, also known as high-intensity ultrasound causes changes in the physical, chemical, or mechanical properties of foods. In the field of biochemistry, ultrasound was initially used to rupture cell walls, releasing their contents. Subsequent studies showed that high-power ultrasound could be used to activate the immobilized enzymes by increasing the rate of transport of substrates to enzymes [12].

From the viewpoint of physics, acoustics has great significance and is defined as the science concerned with the study of acoustic waves, vibrations, and their interactions with the environment [13]. At a microscopical level, sound is an intermolecular collision process and, unlike an electromagnetic wave, a material medium is necessary to propagate an acoustic wave. At a macroscopic level, acoustics refers to variations of pressure depending on time or strain. Eq. (1) represents an acoustic wave:

Table 1 describes the acoustic spectrum of frequencies. As mentioned earlier, acoustic waves require a material medium for propagation, such as a solid or fluid (gaseous or liquid), or a biological medium. There are two main acoustic wave propagation methods: longitudinal and transverse modes. In the longitudinal mode, the emitted acoustic energy propagates in the same direction as the acoustic wave, whereas, in the transversal mode, the emitted acoustic energy propagates vertically to the acoustic wave [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acoustic waves in the ultrasound spectrum
ce:section.ce:para: Acoustic waves may be divided into classes defined by their spectrum of frequencies. The US spectrum covers the frequency range between 20 kHz and 1 GHz. There are two main uses for US acoustic waves: non-destructive and destructive testing. In the former, inspection tests are carried out to determine the acoustic properties of the material and to explore and determine any fractures and deterioration that exist in it. Moreover, non-destructive testing has a broad field of application in diagnostic medicine [15]. By contrast, destructive tests are used to dispose of very specific objects, such as tools used in medicine [16]. However, another significant parameter can be found here: acoustic intensity (W/m2), as the combination of US and increased acoustic intensity can cause two effects, acoustic cavitation and shock waves, by modulating the frequency [17]. The acoustic wave at steady state US frequency generates a phenomenon known as acoustic cavitation. This takes place during wave compression and expansion (See Fig. 1 ) [18,19].

There are many methods for generating acoustic cavitation, through shock waves or steady state. There is a wide field of knowledge on this phenomenon and it continues to be an area of research, with continuous discussions on the description of the phenomenon in the areas of sonoluminescence, sonophysics, and sonochemistry. The phenomenology of acoustic cavitation in liquids is possible thanks to the generation of acoustic waves in the US spectrum in the pulsing and steady state. Acoustic cavitation is the growth and collapse of vapour bubbles embedded in a fluid (liquid) medium. In a steady state, with high acoustic intensity and increased energy in the fluid, a change in state takes place due to elevated temperature and the phenomenon occurs when pressure in a fluid decreases rapidly, creating small bubbles; then, when the fluid pressure increases again, the bubbles implode [15,20].

The concept of acoustic cavitation is used to describe the formation of only one or a group of clouds of bubbles by means of an acoustic field. There are two types of acoustic cavitation, steady and transient cavitation. Steady acoustic cavitation is the process of bubble generation from smaller bubbles, at areas in the acoustic field where nucleation (via impurities) takes place in the fluid. On the other hand, transient acoustic cavitation refers to pressure variation over short timespans and molecular bond rupture in the fluid, causing rapid increases in temperature (Fig. 2 ). Amplitude at the valley of the pressure wave in a steady state, prior to the transient state, corresponds to the negative semi-cycle of pressure generated by the shock wave (transient state). This always takes place during pressure wave expansion in a steady state. The effect of acoustic cavitation in a transient state is the result of the inability of the fluid to tolerate higher pressures during wave expansion; when this occurs, a huge concentration of acoustic energy is released in a very small volume. The origin of acoustic cavitation in the transient state is dependent on the wave amplitude, frequency (pulse width) and profile [21].

If a fluid (liquid) is under tensile stress, the bubbles within it become larger, generating macroscopic bubbles within the fluid at high tensile stress levels. Gaseous cavities increase in size until they achieve their maximum volume. Thus, when the wave goes from peak to valley during pressure, the potential energy obtained during growth is transformed into kinetic energy during implosion. Cavities collapse until bubble sizes smaller than those generated originally are obtained. Implosions of neighbouring bubbles at an interface are asymmetrical and create microjets of fluid that affect the interface (See Fig. 3 ). Mean speed of microjets range from 100 to 340 m/s, being dependent of the pressure profile and initial bubble diameter, which can range from 10 to 100 µm [22].

The most important characteristics of meat quality with strong impact on the industry are pH, water holding capacity (WHC), texture, oxidative stability and sensory attributes [23-27]. When considering the use of emerging technologies in meat, both, the mechanisms of action and the effects on food transformation, preservation, and integrity must be known.

pH is one of the most important indicators of raw meat quality, because it directly affects protein stability and properties. A decrease in pH causes a shrink of the network of polypeptide chains, which decreases the water holding capacity. When the drop in pH is relatively rapid, the changes in myofibrillar and sarcoplasmic proteins result in a decreased WHC [28]. Some meat characteristics linked to WHC include color, texture, firmness, juiciness, and tenderness. The release of water droplets from the muscle originated from the extracellular water is known as drip loss. It is easy to extract and it depends on the state of contraction after rigor mortis because of reduction in the filamentary space and changes in the cellular membrane, which causes the release of water to the extracellular space in the form of drops through the cutting surfaces [24]. Color is a key factor in meat quality because it is the first sensory characteristic assessed by the consumer [29]. In red meat, a bright red color is related to freshness and therefore consumer acceptance [30]. Meat color and exterior appearance may be associated with aging time, shelf life, hardness, and juiciness. Tenderness in meat is determined by its texture. Tenderness is one of the most important attributes of meat quality and it is affected by the composition, structural organization and integrity of the skeletal muscle. The two structural components that determine the intrinsic muscle strength are myofibrillar proteins and connective tissues [31] and the nature of these two components makes difficult to achieve tenderness. Collagen is the most abundant protein of the intramuscular connective tissue, and it is found in various forms in tissues performing various functions depending on its location [32]. It is believed that the amount and chemical composition of collagen contribute to the toughness of meat after an optimal aging time [33]. It has been suggested that any treatment that may cause changes in collagen stability due to physical disruption of collagen fibers or by further enzymatic degradation of the muscle could improve the quality of the meat and the texture properties.

The use of ultrasound in the meat industry began with the evaluation of live cattle fat and muscle. It has been conducted since the 1950s. Nowadays, low-intensity ultrasound is routinely used to improve quality, taste, and tenderness, which represent the most important quality attributes in consumer satisfaction. Many recent studies have reported potential uses of high-intensity ultrasound on fresh meat. Applications have been published with interesting advantages in freezing [34], thawing [35], meat brining [12], cooking [3], bacterial inhibition [36] and tenderizing [37]. The resulting changes of the application of ultrasound to fresh meat are summarized in Table 2 .

The latest research (2015–June 2018, see Table 2) seeking to elucidate the effects of high intensity ultrasound (HIU) on meat or its products, has mainly focussed on bovine muscles, Longissimus and Semitendinosus predominantly [36–46]. Some other studies have been developed using chicken (meat and protein suspension), pork, fish (surimi gels) and rabbit meat [47–54]. The majority of these studies focus on the effect of HIU on microscopic changes, texture, water and salt dynamics in the tissue. Color, sensory characteristics and oxidative stability are other traits of importance when ultrasonicating meat or meat products. Ultrasonication devices vary from cylindrical probes and tanks to cooking stew pots. The ultrasound frequency is not highly variable, 20–40 kHz. However, intensities are highly variable (from 2.39 to 109.7 W/cm2), as well as treatment times (from 1 to 120 min). Despite the recent advances that have been made in the field, research results are still variable. Some findings seem to position HIU as a promising technology for the meat industry, while others stress the need for more research, particularly when the effect interacts with other variables in the tissue. There is a remarkable effect of ultrasound on tenderness and water dynamics of the tissue. Yet, sometimes this effect is positive (softening and retaining water), and some other times it is negative (toughening and releasing water). Particular details about are described along this Section 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High intensity ultrasound on physicochemical characteristics of meat and meat products

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasound effect on pH has been analyzed in various studies and conditions. Some authors have reported that initial pH of meat can increased as a result of ultrasound treatment (2.6 MHz, 10 W/cm2) prior to rigor mortis [55], with no differences in final pH while other reported small differences [56] or no differences in pH (beef M. Semimembranosus) because of ultrasound (2 W/cm2, 45 kHz) after 24, 48, 72 and 96 h postmortem [56,57], nor after beef thawing (25 kHz, 2 W/cm2) [58], or when high intensity ultrasound (24 kHz, 12 W/cm2) was applied to different beef muscles (Longissimus lumborum et thoracis and Semitendinosus muscles) [59] The increase of pH was attributed to the release of ions from the cellular structure into the cytoplasm or to changes in protein structure, which could lead to changes in the position of ionic functionalities that could change the muscle pH.

Numerous studies have been conducted to develop methods to improve tenderness. Among these, ultrasound application methods have been used at various sonication times (From 33 s to 90 m), frequencies (from 15 to 130 kHz), and intensities (from 1.89 to 64 W/cm2). Most authors agree that ultrasound increases meat tenderness [37,39,60–63] (See Fig. 4 ) and shortens the aging period without compromising other quality parameters [37,44,56,64]. The effect was mainly attributed to mechanical rupture of myofibrillar protein structures [56], fragmentation of collagen macromolecules, migration of proteins and other compounds. Thereby accelerating proteolysis or protein denaturation with an increase of desmin and troponin-T degradation, and myofiber fracture along of Z-lines and I-bands, when meat is either sonicated in baths or by probes [11,45]. These effects have also been related to the activation of proteolysis by the release of lysosome cathepsins and/or intracellular calcium ions that activate calpains [39,61] and a shortened aging period [65]. Ultrasound had a significant effect on the characteristics of collagen [66], especially its thermal properties, without having an effect on the insoluble collagen content. Likewise, effects of ultrasound on collagen solubility during cooking have been observed [39], when meat was sonicated into baths (40 kHz and 1500 W). Although some reports indicate that ultrasound has no effect on shear force [31,67–69], it seems that ultrasound does indeed exert a key effect on meat tenderization.

As it is known, the application of ultrasound causes acoustic cavitation which is the phenomenon of generation, growing and eventual collapse of the bubbles which causes the thermal, mechanical, and chemical effects. Mechanical effects include collapse pressure, turbulences due to acoustic streaming, and shear stresses [70], while the chemical effects that include the formation of radicals as a result of critical temperature and pressure conditions are responsible for changes in food compounds. These hydroxyl radicals have been suggested to be used for the oxidative degradation of organic pollutants in an aqueous system [71]. It is possible that both, physical and chemical effects, take place in the meat sonicated surface inducing modifications in tenderness and other physical properties.

Last five years research findings widely agree that HIU has a positive effect on micro-structural changes that potentially lead to higher tenderness. Micro-structural changes of the tissue as a result of HIU show an improved meat texture, either, when HIU is applied on its own [37,39,44,48,50,51,53,54], when HIU is combined with other technologies such as marination [40–42,46,47,49,52,72], or in combination with extrinsic enzyme addition such as papain [43,45]. Deeper later research have shown that micro-structural changes include separation and rupture of myofibrils through z- and m-lines [40,46,51], desmin and troponin-N degradation [41,44], changes in secondary structures of myofibrillar proteins [38,53,72], and myosin denaturation [48]. Depending on the frequency of the ultrasound, locally produced alternating positive and negative pressures cause expansion or compression of the material, resulting in cell rupture [73]. In addition, HIU also promotes a higher activity of either extrinsic and intrinsic enzyme such as calpains [43,45], which can accelerate and increase the structural changes mentioned above particularly during brining procedures, leading to autolysis of myofibril components and the subsequent tenderization [44].

On cured meat, HIU has been considered as an emerging technology, suggesting that micro-channels, micro-agitation and free radicals generated by HIU cavitation contribute to an accelerated mass transport and to structural changes in the meat. It has been reported on beef cured with NaCl and exposure times of 30 and 120 min with power ultrasound (150 and 300 W, 20 kHz), changes in the water retention capacity and the tenderness of the meat compared to traditional curing (P <0.05). This has been confirmed with SDS-PAGE analysis the polymerization of myosin by oxidation, contributing to higher water retention and changes in the alteration of myofibrils by transmission electron microscopy suggesting an increase in the values of myofibril fragmentation index and desmin and troponin proteolysis [41]. Within the methods of preservation of food, the drying process is one of the most popular methods, but it has been observed that it has negatively influence the quality of the product. In addition, due to the high heat capacity of the water, drying is usually a prolonged and energy-intensive process, which is why new drying techniques are constantly being sought. It has been seen that the application of HIU as emerging technology could be used to intensify the curing of dry ham and could improve the texture [74,75]. Recently, HIU has been also studied as a feasible technique for shortening the heating time in the dry curing of ham and improve its texture as an alternative to hot water treatments. There is reported an increase in the apparent thermal diffusion (37%) with high temperatures (50 °C) causing an improvement in the texture of the ham [76].

Studies reporting negative effect of HIU remain scarce. Sonication can be an effective method to improve the technological properties of muscle without effect on lipid oxidation [56]. In a study when analyzing samples treated with ultrasound (45 kHz, 2 W/cm2 for 120 s) and stored in refrigeration, the observed values of substances reactive to 2-thiobarbituic acid (TBARS) that do not compromise the oxidative stability of the meat [77].

Some reports from the last three years regarding the effect of HIU on oxidative stability of meat could provide an explanation for the reduction of tenderness [46,47,52]. When evaluating the oxidative stability, most studies agree that HIU encourages oxidation of both, lipid and protein components. Lipid oxidation normally leads to the production of off-flavours and off-odours, therefore negatively impacting sensory characteristics [78]. HIU applied by probe or bath (2.39, 6.23, 11.32 and 20.96 W/cm2 or 40 kHz, 11 W/cm2, respectively) increases carbonyl formation and TBARS values in beef [37,72] and pork [50]. Although lipid oxidation does not have a direct negative effect on texture, it can accelerate protein oxidation by producing very aggressive free radicals (peroxides, hexanal, propanal, malondialdehyde, etc.) [78]. Protein oxidation, in turn, can lead directly to negative effects on tenderness, increasing toughening [79]. HIU has shown to promote protein oxidation by sulfhydryl losses in beef [38,41,72], chicken myofibrillar protein suspension [48], and in Italian salami [50]. When proteins are oxidized in meat, it generally produces disulphide cross-linking and formation of protein aggregates. Theses chemical structures produce physical forces that lead to a tougher meat [79]. HIU can produce myosin or other protein oxidation, which increases the formation of protein aggregates and produces thiol residues that can potentially act as free radicals, accelerating oxidation of other proteins and components in beef [39,41,72]. Possibly, protein oxidation can also lead to a loss of solubility of myofibril components [48]. Beyond to fragmenting collagen or deteriorating cell membranes, ultrasound by itself have shown to produce the formation of free radicals when applied with a US probe at frequency of 20 kHz [80]. As a consequence, it may intensifies the oxidation of the meat during the treatment with ultrasound, due to the increase in the speed of the chemical reactions [4].

Despite the information generated in the last decade, it remains unclear what specific variables interact to give a consistent beneficial effect of HIU on tenderness. Apparently, protein oxidation in beef can be observed after 60 min of HIU (40 kHz, 1500 W) application [39]. In contrast, other reports shown that protein oxidation can occur after 120 min of HIU application or at higher intensities than 6.23 W/cm2 for at least 90 min [41,72]. This agrees with more recent results reporting an increase of toughness in beef after 80 min of sonication, but a tendering effect after 120 min of HIU application (74.76, 93.33 and 109.67 W/cm2 of intensity applied in a stewpot), which was primarily attributed to rupture of myofibrill structures at that same time [46]. Based on this, it seems that a long sonication time or high intensities, may be the possible trigger to undesirable protein oxidation and toughening.

Another degradation effect of ultrasound in a related-material to meat is on permeability, solubility and diffusion coefficients of oxygen through the biaxially oriented polypropylene films [81]. The higher impact on O2 permeance, solubility and diffusion coefficients was observed when sonication was for 6 min and amplitude of 100% (600 W, 20 kHz, 12–260 µm with a 12.7 mm diameter probe) in distilled water. This effect should be taken into account when ultrasonicating polypropylene packed meat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic structural changes in meat caused by HIU
ce:section.ce:para: The texture of the meat is the most important trait of quality for consumer, and that is directly related to the tissue structure [82]. Hence, the study of muscle fibers at different levels (sarcomeres, bands A, I and Z, mitochondrial membrane) has made possible to know the mechanisms that increase the softness of meat, through the implementation of new technologies [83]. Cavitation generates shock waves that damage the muscular structure. That is why in recent years, many studies have tried to elucidate the ability of the HIU to induce disruption of cell membranes, increasing tenderization in meat. Either, by physically weakening of muscle structures or by activating biochemical processes (proteolysis) that release cathepsins and/or Ca2+ ions that activate calpains [55].

Table 2 summarizes the latest microstructural studies in meat due to the effect of the US. The application of the US in pre-rigor and post-rigor periods causes structural modifications, however there is no extra effect than tenderization caused by ageing [55]. Then, HIU has a limited effect on the components of the meat when aged. The effect of the US on the enzymatic diffusion of papain for the artificial softening of beef (Longissimus lumborum), using intensities of 100 W during 20 min caused a greater penetration of the enzyme into the deeper parts of the muscle. A direct relationship was found between the enzymatic activity and the change in the muscular structure, evidencing an irregular disposition and infraction of fibers, rupture of the endomysium of the collagen fibers, greater space and fragmentation and weakening and rupture of myofibrillar proteins throughout the Z line [43].

HIU has shown to produce higher structural density (smaller oil fractions that reduced empty spaces) in fat-oil pre-emulsions. Rheology, moisture retention and lipid stabilization in chicken breast myofibrillar protein gels improved markedly and the extracted protein efficiently coated the fat, when there is HIU exposition [84].

HIU assisted technology improves tenderness in cured beef by breakdown of Z lines and myofibrils as the HIU power increased. NaCl induced swelling of fibers by binding Cl to protein filaments, that increase the electrostatic repulsive forces, the mechanical effect by microchannels and microagulation generated by the cavitation phenomenon caused these changes. Myofibrils darkened with degradation of myofibrillar sarcomere and myofilamentary network and the low content of NaCl for curing favored myofibrillar fragmentation [41]. Furthermore, HIU increases the absorption of water and NaCl in cured meat; the space between fibers is directly proportional to the ultrasonic intensity and high concentrations of salt are strongly related to the rupture of myofibrils [40].

Mass transfer in cured meat is accelerated by HIU. However, high intensities cause denaturation and protein extraction due to the high cavitation phenomenon. Optical microscopy showed that the HIU did not cause microstructural changes. Nevertheless, cavitation mechanisms work at microscale, so the ultrastructural changes may not be visible with the optical microscope [69].

Changes in the structure of myofibrillar proteins (myosin) of chicken myofibrils treated with US, modifying the gelling properties. HIU decreases dense aggregation and improves WHC of muscle proteins [85]. Furthermore, improved protein functionality in suspensions of PSE chicken breast paste by sonication. HIU increased gel strength, elasticity-viscosity, WHC, uniformity and compactness. The proteins had fewer α-helixes and more folded β sheets [86]. Finally, HIU inactivates ATPase activity, increases the content of sulfhydryl groups and decreases α-helixes and filamentous aggregates. Gelation of actomyosin increased and improved the three-dimensional network and WHC, so the HIU is a promising technology to modify properties of muscle proteins and develop food products of better quality [87].

The effects of ultrasonic treatments on WHC and drip loss are highly variable. Some authors report that ultrasound reduced WHC of rabbit meat treated with 2.25 W/cm2 for 20 min [54]. Cooking loss increased when beef semitendinosus was sonicated for several times (10, 20, 30, 40, 50 or 60 min) at 40 kHz and 1500 W) [39]. Whereas, other authors found reduction on drip loss in broiler breast when sonicated during marination for 20 min in an ultrasonic cleaner [88], beef Semitendinosus sonicated for 60 and 90 min at 40 kHz, 11 W/cm2 [36] and in beef semimembranosus after thawing (25 kHz, 2 W/cm2) [58]. In contrast, some reports indicate that ultrasonicated meat has a higher WHC [39,64,67], similar to that of meat at an advanced postmortem stage. This could be explained by structural changes in myofibrillar proteins caused by ultrasound [56], and the extent of the changes might depend on species, muscle, fibre type, fibre orientation, biochemical factors and ultrasound characteristics. Recently, when high-intensity ultrasound applied during brining of beef, higher WHC were found, possibly by a higher diffusion of salt into the tissue, which can increase the capacity to hold the water before cooking the meat [41,44]. The variability seen in the literature could result from differences in the ultrasound methods; the authors employed various times and intensities, which hindered direct comparison.

Hence, water dynamics in ultrasonicated meat represents another point of disagreement. Recent research may help to elucidate when HIU negatively or positively affects the dynamics of water in meat. The evidence in the last years suggests that meat exposed to HIU without a brining solution, has higher water losses (exudate, by pressure or cooking), either during storage or processing [39,47,48,54]. Time of sonication is an important variable to reduce water losses. If sonication time is short (10–20 min), water holding capacity (WHC) is higher in chicken, whereas the effect is opposite if time is increased to 40 min [47]. Meanwhile, when meat is exposed to HIU during brining (salt addition), the ability of tissue to keep water is improved, increasing coefficient diffusion and WHC [40,41]. This is partially explained by a better diffusion of salts that link water to the tissue. In brining, ultrasonication intensity also plays a significant role. If intensities are higher than 93.3 W/cm2, cooking losses escalate, despite the addition of NaCl to the brine solution [46].

Recently, WHC particularly in myofibrillar proteins have been investigated. Some authors reported that after 10 min of ultrasonication (100 W), the WHC of beef myofibrillar proteins was increased from 45% to 53%. Furthermore, if either intensity or time of sonication increases, WHC can be improved very close to 90% (30 min to 300 W), a remarkable improvement of water retention by the structural proteins of meat [38]. A possible explanation could be that increased sonication intensities cause more vigorous microscale mixing and turbulence, which causes solutes to diffuse [89]. In contrast, myofibrillar proteins suspension from chicken breast had higher water losses (around twice) when ultrasonicated at 20 kHz, 450 W and an amplitude of 60% for 6 min [48]. Possibly, the differences were due to the nature of the sample and the methodology to determine WHC after cooking (by centrifugation vs. cotton filtering, respectively).

Meat color is one of the most important visual characteristics for consumers and producers. Particularly in fresh beef, a cherry-red color is imperative for it to be accepted by consumers. Some reports suggest that ultrasound has no effect on meat color because the heat generated is insufficient to denature proteins and pigments [90]. Conversely, in an assessment of the effect of ultrasound (22 W/cm2) on meat, it has been found that the color changed to a lighter, less red and more yellow-orange color (greater hue angle), which was less bright than control meat [61,67]. Ultrasound accelerates total changes in color, limits the formation of oxymyoglobin, and slows down the formation of metmyoglobin (Stadnik and Dolatowski, 2011). Nevertheless, when meat is cooked, meat panelists do not detect differences between ultrasonicated and control meat [37,42].

Recently, a few studies have been conducted on the effects of HIU on the color of meat fresh. In beef, HIU seems to reduce a* and increase luminosity (L*), which maybe be considered as a negative effect [36]. Other authors, recently reported not change in beef color, when it is ultrasonicated either, in bath (45 kHz, 500 W, 1.8 W/cm2) or with probe (20 kHz, 750 W, 26.5 W/cm2) at 0, 60, 120 or 240 s [91]. In pork products such as Italian salami, HIU did not have an effect on heme pigments or metmyoglobin, which was considered as a positive effect [50]. In fresh rabbit meat, HIU also produces an increase of L*, but reduces the negative effect of NaCl concentrations (70, 140 m and 200 g/L) on meat redness [54]. HIU effects on the color of fresh beef have been considered a disadvantage [92]. Nonetheless, HIU effects on meat of other species than bovine seem uncommon. Based on updated research results, HIU appears to be a forthcoming technology for application in cooked beef, pork and rabbit, where HIU application does not negatively affect color. It may be necessary to further study the effects of HIU application, and the combined effect with other technologies, on beef or other meat species.

Meat marinades may contain salt in two forms: dry or wet. The effect of power ultrasound on pork during wet marination depends on the ultrasound intensity applied. Ultrasound causes bubbles formation that hits the tissue, which may lead to microinjection of brine into the sample. This effect may help to explain the observed increase of NaCl content in the ultrasonicated meat [12,93]. Ozuna et al. [93] evaluated the effect of high intensity ultrasound and NaCl concentration on the brining kinetics (5 ± 1 °C) of pork loin as well as its influence on the textural and microstructural changes. They observed that the NaCl and moisture effective diffusivities were improved by ultrasound application. They also found that the final NaCl and moisture content and the ultrasound application promoted changes in meat texture, which were confirmed via microstructural observations. Ultrasonic treatment (low-intensity and low-frequency) has also been combined with the use of vacuum and it was reported to cause favorable microstructural changes in pork loins marinated in sodium chloride [63]. Factors that modulate the effects of HIU application include ultrasound intensity, time of exposure, processing volume, and sample composition [12,63,94]. The cavitation produced by HIU (4–19 W/cm2, 25–40 min) is independent of temperature effects representing an advantage in the curing process as it was reported by McDonnell et al. [95]. They used a one-directional modelling method to elucidate the mechanistic actions of ultrasonic enhanced mass transfer and proved the potential of power US for the accelerated salting of pork.

HIU affects denaturation and aggregation of collagen fibers in the extracellular space of meat structure, with no change in luminosity and redness of meat [39]. In a recent work beef was cured with NaCl and ultrasonicated (150 and 300 W, 20 kHz) for 30 and 120 min and changes in the water holding capacity and the tenderness of the meat compared to traditional curing were observed [40]. Confirming with SDS-PAGE analysis the polymerization of myosin by oxidation, contributing to greater water retention and changes in the alteration of myofibrils suggested an increase in the values of myofibril fragmentation index and desmin and troponin proteolysis.

Meat is rich in nutrients and water, which creates the perfect setting for pathogenic and decomposition microorganism growth. Conventional treatments for microbial inactivation include the use of high temperatures. However, findings have shown that these technologies deteriorate the functional and sensorial properties of food, especially the thermosensitive compounds [96]. In meat, marination with salt is also a common method for controlling microorganisms, as microbial inactivation is achieved through high osmotic activity and decreased water activity. Usually, a container with NaCl at 1.6–2.2% is used. However, to achieve these concentrations within the meat, a longer marinating time is required, which poses higher microbial contamination risks in the case of raw meat. Furthermore, subsequent procedures such as cooking are also a risk as toxins produced by bacteria such as Escherichia coli O157:H7 [97], causing severe uremic hemolytic syndrome, and Bacillus cereus [98], causing gastrointestinal diseases, cannot be destroyed by heat.

High-power (>1 W/cm2 and frequencies between 20 and 500 kHz) ultrasound offers an alternative to the traditional methods of food preservation and is considered a green, versatile, and emerging technology [3,99]. Ultrasound produces cavitation in a liquid medium, contributing to the antimicrobial effect and increasing the shelf life of food [4,41,100]. The antimicrobial effectiveness of high-intensity ultrasound depends on factors such as contact time with the microorganism, microorganism type, food quantity, composition, and treatment temperature [101,102]. Intracellular cavitation produces the thinning of cell membranes, warming and production of free radicals such as H2O2, whose hydroxyl radicals damage the cell membrane and DNA [103,104]. It is suggested that a greater bacterial surface area in larger bacteria can cause higher pressures under treatment by sonic waves as a result of the cavitation phenomenon, making them more vulnerable to the sonication treatment [105]. It is also important to note the significant differences between Gram positive (Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis) and Gram negative (Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli) bacteria [106,107]. According to reports, Gram positive bacteria are more resistant to ultrasound, as the cell wall structure is thicker due to the layer of peptidoglycans protecting the cell from sonication [108,109]. However, a controversy on this matter has arisen, as other researchers have found no differences in Gram positive and negative bacteria counts after treatment with ultrasound. Moreover, cell morphology is suggested to be a major factor, as coccoid-shaped cells have shown greater resistance to sonication than bacillus [110]. Similarly, bacteria that form spores, of Clostridium and Bacillus species, have shown greater resistance to ultrasonic treatment than vegetative cells [41,101,111], these bacteria are more resistant to thermal treatment, as well as to ultrasound.

Table 3 shows the most recent studies where ultrasound has been used for microbial control in meat. The use of ultrasound is particularly important for controlling milk and wine fermentation processes, in which the stimulation of microorganisms such as Bifidobacterium and lactic acid bacteria, and reduction of Brettanomyces yeast levels [112,113] have been observed; studies on meat fermentation are not available.

The inactivation of Salmonella typhimurium, Salmonella derby, Salmonella infantis, Yersinia enterocolitica, and a pathogenic strain of Escherichia coli was studied in inoculated samples [114]. Ultrasound may also be a suitable method for decontaminating poultry carcass skin. Some authors [115] observed a decrease of Gram-negative bacteria (Salmonella anatum, Escherichia coli, Proteus sp., and Pseudomonas fluorescens) or a reduction in the population of Campylobacter on the surface of poultry skin after ultrasonic treatment. The use of ultrasound for bacterial control has been reported to be more effective when combined with other physical (heat or pressure) and chemical (chlorine, lactic acid, red wine) methods for achieving microbial inactivation [100,116]. Several researchers have combined technologies involving ultrasound and steam for effective microorganism reduction, as steam affects bacteria in the microstructure and cavities; this has been of great relevance in the case of Campylobacter, Enterococcus faecium, and Bacillus subtilis spores, where external membrane permeation of microorganisms and their spores take place. Ultrasound and steam (sonovapour, 90–94 °C and 30–40 kHz for 10 s) used in chicken carcasses contaminated with Campylobacter reduced bacteria counts significantly. Treating chicken carcasses immediately after slaughter with steam and ultrasound in the processing line, ultrasound reduces Campylobacter counts by approximately three logarithmic units [117]. The antimicrobial power of ultrasound is boosted at 60 and 70 °C, as the cell structure of microorganisms at these temperatures is more susceptible to physical actions, improving the effects of sonication [118]. Nevertheless, various researchers report that temperatures between 10 and 20 °C intensify the cavitation phenomenon, allowing the formation of reactive oxygen species and shock forces. Treating fresh beef with a power ultrasound method decreased its bacterial load, particularly of coliforms and psychrophilic bacteria with a greater reduction during storage [36].

Recently, the impact of ultrasound (20, 40 and 100 kHz. And power of 10.5 W/cm2) on the growth reduction of the strain of Salmonella enterica subs. Typhimurium was demonstrated [119]. Likewise, the effects of ultrasound on the inactivation of microorganisms during the curing processing has being confirmed by other authors. The particle size distribution of bacterial and cell fluorescence staining analysis showed that ultrasound could result in the formation of cell fragments through destroying the integrity of the membrane of E. coli O157:H7 and Bacillus cereus [120] .

Despite all the previous results, it may be considered that HIU effect on meat microbiota is still not conclusive. Regarding this, Piñon et al. [121] found that ultrasonication to chicken breast (40 kHz, 9.6 W/cm2, 30 and 50 min), increased mesophilic, psychrophilic, lactic acid bacteria and Staphylococcus aureus populations after 7 d of meat storage at 4 °C. Hence, some researches have studied coupled technologies to HIU, aiming to obtain better results on bacterial inactivation. Mikš- Mikš-Krajnik et al. [122] found that combining HIU/UV light and HIU/UV light/electrolysed acid water, significantly reduced Listeria monocytogenes and natural microbiota in Salmon previously contaminated with 105 log UFC/g of L. monocytogenes. In this case, HIU was applied to salmon filets + 100 ml of distilled water, inside stomacher® bags for 1 min in an ultrasonic bath (Elma®, 45 kHz and 200 W). Besides, Condón-Abanto et al. [123] reported that HIU-assisted cooking (55 L tank, 900 W, 75 °C, 45 min) of crab can be reduced cooking time up to 15%, improving as well the rate and amount of released compounds. Additionally, HIU assisted cooking also decreased microbial populations, due to a better heat penetration on the crab structure.

Ultrasound has also been applied on the freezing of meat where ultrasound can induce nucleation at a higher temperature during the freezing process. This effect can be used to control the size and distribution of ice crystals or reducing time required in frozen foods [35,124,125]. Some reports evidenced that the application of ultrasound can fragment the ice crystals, which increases the number of nuclei and reduces the size of the ice crystals. Most studies have been conducted on fruits and vegetables [126–130], fish [131] and gluten [132]. In pork, the ultrasound-assisted freezing effect showed an improvement in the freezing rate and the quality of the longissimus muscle during freezing by immersion [133]. In this study, a uniform distribution of the ice crystals was observed in samples treated with an ultrasound power of 180 W compared to other evaluated ultrasound powers (120, 240 and 300 W).

The effects of ultrasound on the meat tissue are not expected to be homogenous. Most probably the acoustic cavitation effects observed on the surface of the meat products are different than those in the interior sections of meat where acoustic cavitation effects differ. The ultrasonic baths do not generate a homogeneous pattern of emission of high intensity acoustic waves in the ultrasound spectrum, because it has one or several acoustic emitters, which has a spatial emission function of the sine-cardinal type, as a consequence of a system in the form of a piston with baffle; likewise, when there is a set of acoustic emitters, they have an overlap of intensities of the same type of emission [18].

When submitting a meat cut, whether it is packaged or unpackaged in the ultrasonic bath, the effect of acoustic cavitation will be inhomogeneous, due to the aforementioned effects and the position of the meat with respect to the acoustic emitter (s), where the near and far field effects have a fundamental role [13]. The acoustic cavitation has very little effect within the structure of the meat, the modifications of the meat structures is a consequence of the increase of the temperature and the shock waves generated by the implosion of the bubbles [134].

Selected and potential applications of ultrasound mainly in the field of food preservation and product modification were discussed. High-intensity ultrasound generates acoustic cavitation in a liquid medium, developing physical forces that are considered the main mechanism responsible for the observed changes in exposed materials. The effect of HIU on meat and meat products is beyond doubt. A moderate range of time exposure and intensity of sonication increases tenderness and reduces shear force; either, by generating micro-structural changes in the tissue or increasing enzyme activity. HIU reduces microbial loads in meat, mainly when combined with other technologies such as; termic treatments, UV light, electrolyzed acid water, steam, essential oils etc, resulting in the destruction of living cells and this effect remains during cold storage. Nowadays approaches of HIU have been also focused on microbial control in processes that benefit from bacterial activities, such as fermentation from Lactobacillus sakei in foods. It is well documented that the US modifies the biochemical, functional and structural properties of the proteins, however, most of the studies use ultrasonic processors with a probe, leaving ultrasonic baths aside as a source of ultrasonic energy. The latest research also improves the understanding of how an inadequate sonication time may negatively affect texture, possibly by an oxidative instability of proteins leading to crosslinking. HIU increases WHC in meat when it is combined with other technologies such as marination. By itself, HIU seems to increase water losses in fresh meat, possibly from water not strongly bound to the structural proteins in the tissue. Color changes in meat due to HIU exposure may not be highly relevant for cooked or processed beef, neither for other meat species such as chicken, pork and rabbit. Deeper and new research should be lead combining HIU with other innovative technologies such as; high pressures, electric pulses, microwaves and metal nanoparticles, to achieve a more efficient control on microorganisms, but also a more integral benefit on sensorial, nutritional and technological properties. Furthermore, there is still an information gap on how time and intensity may interact with other factors to produce positive or negative results on meat quality.

Major advances have been made towards the understanding of the effects of HIU on the physicochemical properties of meat and its products. HIU is already considered an emerging technology that in some conditions improves the physicochemical quality of meat. However, a better understanding of molecular, microscopic and physical processes around those effects remains necessary. A wider understanding of how this physical force can have a positive effect on the intricate environment in the meat, may lead to the next step of designing procedures and equipment for the application of HIU at industrial levels and to the combination of ultrasound with other technologies to accurately improve the meat quality. The US as assisted technology for the improvement of the quality of meat and its products is increasingly close to scaling at the industrial level. There is still more way to go for the development of US equipment for the fresh meat industry, because the complex matrix of protein-fat makes the transfer of the US difficult, however it is likely that the advance in cured meat products and emulsified, including the extraction of certain components, allows finding this type of products on the market in the short term. High intensity ultrasound needs to be developed and scaled up for its application on online processes.
The authors declare that they have no interest or benefit arising from the direct applications of this chapter.
